metadata.title: Conducting, Not Prompting: The Gru/Minion Split
metadata.description_blurb: The conductor on a small box doesn't play better than the cellist — she holds the piece. Claude holds none of it. The Gru/Minion split is the discipline that makes this work at any scale.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: Seth spent four hours in one session. He kept prompting — adding corrections, adding context, pasting outputs back. The login function grew more complex. The test suite grew to cover the corrections. By hour four the code passed all tests and did something Seth could not fully explain. He had been prompting. He had not been conducting. The difference is where the deciding happened. This is Chào, Liam — in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, implement login function session

props.command: implement the login function and add tests for the edge cases

beats.narration_text: The conductor is not better than the cellist at the cello. She is not better than the violinist at the violin. What she does — the entire reason there is a person on a box — is hold the piece. She has the score as a whole. She knows what bar 47 is supposed to feel like against bar 12. The orchestra plays exactly what they understood her to mean. The gap between what she meant and what they understood is where everything breaks. You are the conductor. Claude is the orchestra.
beats.new_visual_element: Conductor model — you hold the piece / Claude executes the parts / gap = what you specified vs. what Claude understood

beats.narration_text: The Minion Part: typing, boilerplate, syntactic resolution, idiomatic translation, running tests. High-volume, fast, error-prone in specific ways, superhuman in most. With clear instructions they execute faster than you could alone. Without instructions they wreck things at speed. The Gru Part: deciding. Writing the specification. Writing the handoff condition. Reading the result against the world. The Gru Part is what makes the work yours. If you delegate it, you watched the thing get built — you did not build it.
beats.new_visual_element: Gru/Minion table — Gru: deciding / spec / criteria. Minion: typing / pattern / tests

beats.narration_text: Seth's second attempt: he closed the first session without committing. Ten minutes of Gru work. He wrote the data model — what an application record contains, in writing, before Claude sees it. Named the boundary — what the login function owns and what it does not. Wrote the handoff condition — the specific tests that define pass. Then he prompted. Same Claude. Same minute. Same model. Different output — because the conductor held the piece before the orchestra started.
beats.new_visual_element: Before second attempt — data model / boundary / handoff condition all decided before first prompt

beats.narration_text: Boondoggling is what happens when the Gru Part gets delegated. The session fills with corrections and re-prompts. Quality degrades. The context contains the failed attempts. The output becomes something the student cannot explain — because the deciding happened inside the model, not inside the student. Conducting is the discipline that keeps the deciding in the human lane. Your wrists will be less tired than your head. That is the correct distribution.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — conducting = Gru before session / boondoggling = Gru during session

beats.narration_text: Do the Gru Part before your next coding session. Paste this.

props.command: Before I start coding: help me do the Gru Part. For this task — [describe it] — ask me: (1) what does the data model look like, in writing, before you generate any code, (2) what is the explicit boundary of this function — what does it own and what does it not, (3) what is the handoff condition — the specific, runnable test that defines pass. Do not generate any code until I have answered all three.

beats.narration_text: If this keeps the deciding in your lane, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
